title.en: Owning a failure that wasn't strictly your fault
title.es: Asumir la responsabilidad de un fallo que no fue estrictamente tu culpa

question.en: Describe a failure you owned that wasn't strictly your fault but that you took responsibility for improving. What did you do, and what did you learn?
question.es: Describe un fallo que asumiste como tuyo que no fue estrictamente tu culpa, pero del que tomaste la responsabilidad de mejorar. ¿Qué hiciste y qué aprendiste?

must_include: a concrete failure with a specific impact — not a vague setback, but a named consequence (downtime, data loss, missed deadline, customer impact)
clarity on why the candidate was not the primary cause — acknowledging the context without using it as an excuse
the candidate's personal decision to own the failure improvement rather than waiting for someone else to fix it
a specific systemic change the candidate made — not just apologizing or monitoring more, but changing a process, tool, or guardrail
the lesson carried forward — a behavior or decision heuristic the candidate adopted as a result
must_avoid: framing that subtly blames the original fault owner ('I had to clean up after my teammate's mistake')
stopping at acknowledgment without showing what was changed — 'I owned it and communicated clearly' is insufficient
fabricating total blame ownership when the actual story was shared — interviewers detect when candidates overclaim ownership
